coredata.dc:title: Effect of different aqueous electrolytes on electrochemical behavior of LiFePO4 as a cathode material: Lithium ion battery and renewable energy nexus
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Sukesh
Chand, Prakash
Kumar, Anand
Anand, Hardeep
coredata.dc:description: Lithium iron phosphate as a cathode source is synthesized by a simple hydrothermal synthesis route and its electrochemical performance in different aqueous electrolytes, such as 2M NaOH, 1M Na2SO4, 1M KOH, and 3M KOH is investigated. X-ray diffraction revealed orthorhombic olivine structure with Pbnm (62) space group and lattice parameters a = 4.98 Å, b = 10.04 Å, and c = 5.89 Å. Field emission scanning electron microscopy analysis shows disc form morphology with a particle size distribution in the range of 150 nm -600 nm. In the Raman spectroscopy, the sample's intense peaks at 1390 cm−1 wavenumber indicate the higher ratio of disordered carbon deposit as carbanions material deposited on the LiFePO4 particles.The cyclic voltammetry study confirms the redox reactivity of the LFP cathode and is used to measure the diffusion coefficient of the carrier ion for each electrolyte.Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy is used to predict the chemical kinetics and mass-limitations in the cell.Experimental results showed that the electrochemical efficiency of LiFePO4 can be significantly affected the electrolytes, the maximum peak current, diffusivity, the charge transfer resistance is optimal in case of 1M KOH in comparison to Na2SO4 as well as NaOH.
coredata.dcterms:subject: LiFePO4
Aqueous electrolytes
Hydrothermal
Electrochemical properties
Lithium-ion batteries

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The crystal structure of olivine LiFePO4 in projection along [001]. [13]
Schematic of a lithium-ion battery, electrolyte permits the diffusion of Li-ions in between the cathode and anode and the electrons are pushed out to do external work. [15]
Energy band diagram of an electrolyte window (Eg) and the electrode electrochemical potentials μA(anode)and μC(cathode). [8]
(a). Schematic diagram of the synthesis procedure of LiFePO4 cathode material via hydrothermal technique (b)Schematic diagram of the preparation of the LiFePO4 electrode.
(a). Room temperatureXRD patterns of as-synthesized LiFePO4 compared with standard LiFePO4 (JCPDS card No.77-0179 and 40-1499) (b). The Williamson-Hall plot (W-H plot) for the LiFePO4 XRD peaks.
FESEM image analysis of the as-synthesized LiFePO4 sample. (a) 10000x magnification (b) 40000x magnification(c) particle size distribution.
FTIR spectrum for the as-synthesized LiFePO4 sample.
Raman spectrum of LiFePO4 sample after deconvolution of D and G band.

(a). Cyclic voltammetry plots of LFP with differnt electrolytes(a) 2M NaOH (b) 1M Na2SO4 (c) 1M KOH (d) Comparison of the peak values of the redox current for the LiFePO4 cathode with (a) 1M KOH, (b) 1M Na2SO4 and (c) 2M NaOH electrolytes (e) Anodic peak current variation of the LiFePO4 cathode with respect to the voltage sweeping frequency for the three electrolytes (f) Cyclic voltammograms comparison of the cases when 1M KOH and 3M KOH is used as an electrolyte.

(a) EIS complex plane plot for the different electrolytes with the frequency in the range of 0.1 Hz – 100KHz, inset shows the zoomed version of high-frequency variation for the case of 1M KOH and 2M NaOH as an electrolyte (b) Electrical circuit representation for the Kinetic and mass-transfer behavior of the LiFePO4 active cathode region, passivation layer, electrolyte, and different interfaces associated with these (c) The 3D plot of the EIS data where the x-axis is the frequency in log-scale, the y-axis is the real part of impedance ZRE and the z-axis is the negative of the imaginary part of the impedance ZIMG.

(a). The charge-discharge curve of LiFePO4 cathode with 2M NaOH as an electrolyte (b)The charge-discharge curve of LiFePO4 cathode with 1M Na2SO4 as an electrolyte (c) The charge-discharge curve of LiFePO4 cathode with 1M KOH as an electrolyte.

Variation of electrochemical kineticparameters for LEP cathode material with different electrolytes, ΔV (V) is the difference between the anodic and cathodic peaks potential, ipa (mA) is the anodic peak current, DLi+(cm2s−1) is the diffusion coefficient, Rct(Ω) is the charge transfer resistance, Rsol(Ω)is the resistance due to electrolyte solution, and ΔV (V) is the voltage difference between the charge-discharge plateaus.
Nomenclature of the reporting symbols used in the equations and their unit of measurement.

row.entry: Peak broadening due to crystalline size
Peak broadening due to lattice stain
number of electrons in the redox reaction

head.ce:title: Effect of different aqueous electrolytes on electrochemical behavior of LiFePO4 as a cathode material: Lithium ion battery and renewable energy nexus

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, National Institute of Technology, Kurukshetra 136119, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Physics, National Institute of Technology, Kurukshetra 136119, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Integrated & Honors Studies, Kurukshetra University Kurukshetra, 136119, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bInstitute of Integrated & Honors Studies, Kurukshetra University Kurukshetra, 136119, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Kurukshetra University, Kurukshetra-136119 India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bInstitute of Integrated & Honors Studies, Kurukshetra University Kurukshetra, 136119, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium iron phosphate as a cathode source is synthesized by a simple hydrothermal synthesis route and its electrochemical performance in different aqueous electrolytes, such as 2M NaOH, 1M Na2SO4, 1M KOH, and 3M KOH is investigated. X-ray diffraction revealed orthorhombic olivine structure with Pbnm (62) space group and lattice parameters a = 4.98 Å, b = 10.04 Å, and c = 5.89 Å. Field emission scanning electron microscopy analysis shows disc form morphology with a particle size distribution in the range of 150 nm -600 nm. In the Raman spectroscopy, the sample's intense peaks at 1390 cm−1 wavenumber indicate the higher ratio of disordered carbon deposit as carbanions material deposited on the LiFePO4 particles.The cyclic voltammetry study confirms the redox reactivity of the LFP cathode and is used to measure the diffusion coefficient of the carrier ion for each electrolyte.Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy is used to predict the chemical kinetics and mass-limitations in the cell.Experimental results showed that the electrochemical efficiency of LiFePO4 can be significantly affected the electrolytes, the maximum peak current, diffusivity, the charge transfer resistance is optimal in case of 1M KOH in comparison to Na2SO4 as well as NaOH.

ce:section.ce:para: The present LIBs use organic electrolytes, which are volatile and adds to the cost of production, to make renewable energy a more viable option, energy storage device needs to be cheaper, therefore we study the behavior of LiFePO4 electrode under various aqueous electrolyte.

Therefore, we attempt to present a systematic effort to investigate the structural and electrochemical properties of LiFePO4 cathode material synthesized via hydrothermal technique and comparison of the electrochemical behavior of LFP cathode under the three above mentioned electrolytes. The voltage ranges for KOH is 0.0 to 0.56 V, Na2SO4 is -0.2 to 1.2 V, and NaOH is -0.4 to 0.67 V, which are well within the safety limits of aqueous electrolytes (1.5V). The electrochemical analysis is conducted using the electrochemical workstation, a three-electrode device (CH760E). The effects of the different aqueous electrolytes on LEP electrodes at room temperature have been investigated. The highest specific capacity from 1M KOH electrolyte has been accomplished in contrast with Na2SO4 andNaOH electrolytes. Structural analysis of LiFePO4 nanostructures was done using X-ray diffraction, FESEM, FTIR, and Raman spectroscopy.

For the synthesis of LiFePO4 nanostructures via hydrothermal technique, sodium dihydrogen phosphate dihydrate (NaH2PO4)(> 99 %) and lithium acetate (LiOOCCH3) (>98%) were purchased from Merck. Ferric nitrate nonahydrate (Fe(NO3)3.9H2O) (98%), cetrimonium bromide (CTAB) (>98%), potassium hydroxide (KOH) (> 98%), and Sodium sulfate (Na2SO4)(> 98%) were purchased from LOBA. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH)(> 97%) was purchased from SDFCL. In the present work, all the chemicals used are of analytical grade and are used without additional purification.

LiFePO4 (LFP) was synthesized utilizing the hydrothermal technique, as shown in Fig. 4 (a). NaH2PO4 (0.46 g) and Fe(NO3)3.9H2O (1.53 g) solution were formed in 40 milliliters of deionized (DI) water each one in this standard synthesis process, and then the Fe(NO3)3.9H2O solution was mixed dropwise under constant stirring in the NaH2PO4 solution. Meanwhile, in 40 ml DI, a 14 millimolar solution of CTAB was also prepared and stirred for an hour, which was then applied dropwise under constant stirring at room temperature to the first mixed solution. The solution was also allowed for stirring for another hour, then a fine LiOOCCH3 powder weighing 1.16g was transferred along with the final solution into a Teflon lined autoclave and sealed airtight, and held in a hot air oven at 180 °C for 6 h. After that, the autoclave was kept at room temperature for cooling down and then opened after it attained room temperature. From the as described hydrothermal route of synthesis,precipitates of LFP were acquired, washed using the traditional method, and then dried and annealed at a temperature of 800 °C in the furnace. To avoid oxidation in the air, LiOOCCH3 was applied at the last moment.

To estimate the electrochemical properties of the synthesized sample, the working electrode (cathode) was prepared by slurrying composed of 80 wt % active cathode material (as prepared LFP) with PVDF (Polyvinylidene fluoride) 10% and the activated carbon, i.e., AC about 10% in N-Methyl-2-Pyrrolidone solvent (NMP) which was stirred for 18hrs for homogeneity at room temperature. The as-prepared slurry was drop cast on a 1 × 1 cm2on the commercial purchase nickel foil and then dried in the oven at 70 °C for 12hrs as shown in Fig. 4(b).The electrodes were weighed before and after the whole process to measure the cathode composite deposited on the Ni foil.To examine the electrochemical properties of hydrothermal technique-synthesized LFP cathode material and comparison of the electrochemical behavior of LFP cathode in different aqueous electrolytes, KOH (1.12g), Na2SO4 (2.84g), and NaOH (1.6 g)was dissolved in 20ml DI water each to prepare a 1M KOH, 1M Na2SO4, and 2M NaOHaqueous electrolyte.A three-electrode system was utilized with the platinum electrode as a counter electrode, Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode, and the active material (LEP)layered on a nickel foil as a working electrode.

The structure and phase of the as-prepared sample (LEP) were determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku Co., Japan) using a Cu-Kα radiation source (λ = 1.541 Å), with a scanning range of 150–800at a scan rate of 2 0 per minute.The FESEM (FEG-Quanta-450, FEI) was used to analyze the grain size and surface morphology of as-synthesized LiFePO4 (LFP) cathode material. For FESEM analysis, firstly, the pulverized sample (LEP) wascaked with a layer of gold to make it conducting. Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectrum of the pulverized sample (LEP) was carried out using Perkin Elmer IR Spectrophotometer in 400–3500 cm−1 at room temperature. The structural variations for the carbonaceous material and phase formation of as-prepared LEP cathode material were studied by Raman spectroscopy (InVia; Renishaw Raman Microscope, 514 nm Laser).The electrochemical cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of the as-prepared LEP cathode electrode werecarried out with the assist of theelectrochemical workstation (CHI760E, USA) at room temperature. The details of the instruments utilized in the present work is also mentioned in the tabular form as below:
Characterization Instrument
XRD XRD, Rigaku Co., Japan, Cu-Kα radiation source (λ = 1.541 Å)
FESEM FEG-Quanta-450, FEI
FTIR Perkin Elmer IR Spectrophotometer
Raman spectroscopy InVia; Renishaw Raman Microscope, 514 nm Laser
Electrochemical CHI760E workstation

The diffraction peaks of the as-prepared LFP are shown in Fig. 5 (a), along with the XRD peaks for the standard LiFePO4corresponding to JCPDS card no. 77–0179 and 40–1499. All the diffraction peaks for the as-prepared LFP sample can be indexed in an orthorhombic structure with the Pnma space group.The broadening of peaks in the XRD isbecause of the crystallite size,the coherence domain length, and the development of micro strains in the lattice as the particle size is reduced [21]. Total broadening of the XRD peaks (βT), which is measured as FWHM of the diffraction peaks in Fig. 5(a), is the sum of the broadening due to the crystallite size (βD) and the broadening due to strain (βε) as shown in Eq. (1). The crystallite size (D) and the strain (ε) in the LFP lattice structure can be estimated from the Williamson-Hall plot (W-H plot) by using Eqs. (1)–(3) to get Eq. (4) as shown in Fig. 5(b). The crystallite size of the as-synthesized LFP crystals is calculated to be 40 nm, and the strain in the crystal lattice is 7.9 × 10−4. The lattice parameters of LFP orthorhombic crystal as calculated using Eqs. (5)–(6) are a = 4.89Å, b = 10.04Å, and c = 5.89 Å, which are consistent with previous results [22]. Thus, XRD confirms the orthorhombic Phospho-olivine geometry with the Pbnm (62) space group for the LFP material synthesized via hydrothermal technique.

β D

a 2

k 2

l 2
c 2

Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) is used to analyze the morphology of the LFP samples synthesized by the hydrothermal route of synthesis at 180°C and calcined at 800°C. From Fig. 6 (a), it is observed that the as-prepared LFP particles mostly have disc type morphology. For better clarity, a high magnification image of the same LFP sample is shown in Fig. 6 (b); it reveals the particles’ sharp edges and corners, inferring their crystalline appearance. Some agglomeration present in the sample can be attributed to the high-temperature synthesis. There is non-uniformity in the particle size distribution, as shown in Fig. 6 (c). The average size of the as-synthesized LFP particles is in the range of 150 nm -600 nm, which, when viewed along with the crystallite size of 40 nm obtained from the XRD analysis: is indicative of the growth of smaller particles and also further agglomeration of smaller grains.

Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) is done to analyze the lattice dynamics of the as-synthesized LFP material. FTIR treatment of LFP is dominated by fundamental (PO4)3− vibrational modes with the assumption that these modes can be separated into internal modes (PO4)3− and external modes (lattice). The internal modes are attributed to the displacement of oxygen atoms of the tetrahedral (PO4)3− anions which are characterized by peaks at frequencies v 1 = 938 cm−1 (singlet), v 2 = 465 cm−1 (doublet), v 3 = 1027 cm−1 and v 4 = 567 cm−1 (triplets) wherev 1 and v3 represent the symmetric and antisymmetric stretching mode of the P-O bonds, whereas v2 andv 4 represent mainly O-P-O symmetric and antisymmetric bending mode with a small contribution of P vibration [23]. FTIR spectrum for the as-synthesized LFP material is shown in Fig. 7 , it revels the doublet v 2 = 433 cm−1 -518 cm−1; and triplets v 3 = 1031cm−1 -1086cm−1 and v 4 = 601cm−1 -713cm−1. The splitting of fundamental (PO4)3− anion bands is due to the effect of the correlationinduced by the coupling with Fe-O units in the LFP structure [24]. Slight, intense peaks involving the vibration of carbon bonds are present due to the use of CTAB in the synthesis procedure, which also acts as a carbon source.Thus, the FTIR treatment of the as-prepared LFP material identifies the symmetric stretching modes with the appearance of triplets in the high-wavenumber region and doublet in the far-infrared region due to the site-symmetry effect and the correlation effect [25].

Raman spectroscopy is an essential tool to analyze the structural dynamics of LFPolivine crystal structure used as a cathode material for LIB and determine the form of carbanions material (CTAB) deposited on the LFP particles. Raman spectra for various carbanesous materials are reported previously by multiple groups that show two broad lines, approximately 1350 cm−1 and 1585 cm−1, which are characteristic fingerprints of disordered or graphitic carbon [26,27]. In addition to this LiFePO4 is decomposable under local heating due to laser exposure [28] which can be reduced by the carbon layer deposition. But carbon deposition also reduces the intensity of the LiFePO4 structure due to attenuation and overlapping of the spectrum. The Raman spectra of the as-synthesized LFP sample, as shown in Fig. 8 , involves frequencies due to internal vibrations of the (PO4)3- polyanions at V 1 = 961 cm−1(symmetric P-O stretching), V 2 = 506 cm−1 (symmetric O-P-O bond bending)and V 4 = 621 cm−1 (antisymmetric O-P-O bond bending) [29]. The lower frequency peaks observed at 138 cm−1, 217 cm−1, 278 cm−1, 314 cm−1, 372 cm−1, and 395 cm−1 correspond to the translation of Fe and coupled translation and vibrational motion of Fe and PO4 3−, respectively [26]. The Raman absorption bands observed in the range of 900 to 1150 cm−1 correspond to the stretching mode of the PO4 3− unit and occupy symmetric and asymmetric P–O bonds [28–30]. The intense broad bands located at ∼1340 cm−1 - 1580 cm−1are deconvolved into peaks assigned to carbon vibrations relying on similar features in the spectrum of graphite [26]. The peak at 1551 cm−1represents the G-band, which corresponds to the E2g zone center optically allowed mode of crystalline graphite [26, 28]. The peak at 1396 cm−1 represents the D-band attributed to the crystallite's finite size, due to which an A10 mode of the lattice becomes Raman active [31]. The D-band represents the disorder-induced phonon mode or defects, whereas G-band stands for the presence of carbon. The existence of defects is supportive of improving the capability of interfacial lithium storage capacity. Though the Raman spectrum illustrates a sharp carbon peak, the carbon remains in the amorphous state in the LiFePO4, as the sample is treated with CTAB as a surfactant. Hence, it is beneficial to the conductivity properties of LiFePO4 and improves the electrochemical properties of Lithium-ion batteries. Raman spectra of the carbon deposithave been deconvolved into four peaks. Fig. 8 shows intensity peaks at 1197 cm−1 and 1452 cm−1assigned to short-range vibrations of sp3-coordinated carbons as already observed in previous results fordisordered carbon blacks [31–35]. Raman spectroscopy of the as-synthesized LFP sample confirms Raman's active vibrational modes of PO4 3− polyanion of LiFePO4 olivine structure, carbanesous residual (source: CTAB), and decomposition products present due to laser-induced local heating of the sample.

The Electrochemical characterizations of the as-prepared LFP cathode materialwith different electrolytes are done using the CHI760E workstation. It is a three-electrode system with the LFP acting as the cathode, platinum counter electrode as an anode, and Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode, which isplaced in a chamber filled with the electrolyte. The electrochemical test is conducted to investigate the reaction rate and diffusion kinetics of the LFP cathode under the three different electrolytes. Fig. 9 (a–c) shows the cyclic voltammograms (CV) for the LFP cathode with 2M NaOH, 1M Na2SO4,1M KOH electrolytes respectively at a sweep rate of 50 mVs−1. The oxidative and reductive potential sweep for 2M NaOH in Fig. 9 (a) and 1M KOH in Fig. 9(c) gives one point of inflection each corresponding to the anodic and cathodic charge transfer respectively, whereas for the 1M Na2SO4 electrolyte as shown in Fig. 9(b), two anodic peaks are observed during the oxidative sweep and a single peak for thereduction sweep which is characteristic of NaFePO4 [36–41]. LiFePO4 cathode in pure Na2SO4 electrolyte is known to transform into olivine-structured NaFePO4 [33]. The split in the anodic peaks for NaFePO4 can be related to the big volumetric change during its phase transition, as shown below [36].
NaFePO4→Na0.7FePO4→FePO4

The intensity of the CV peaks is correlated to the passivation level at the electrode surface. The lower the passivation level higher the reactivity, and hence the higher the peak current. Fig. 9(d) reveals that the CV peak currents for 1M KOH as an electrolyte have a maximum intensity, which indicates maximum reaction rate and least passivation level among the three different electrolytes. The difference between the anodic and cathodic peaks potential (ΔV) is correlated to the polarization or reversibility of the redox reaction; the smaller the difference, the greater the reversibility and the smaller the polarization more cyclic retentivity [42]. From Fig. 9(a–c) and Table 1 , it is observed thatfor 2M NaOH, ΔV = 0.1 V. For 1M KOH, ΔV = 0.11 V which indicates high redox reversibility for these two electrolytes. In contrast, ΔV = 0.21 V and ΔV’ = 0.34 V for the Na2SO4 electrolyte indicates low reversibility, which can be attributed to the large change in volume during the conversion of NaFePO4 into FePO4 [36]. The CV anodic peak current variation concerning the sweeping voltage frequency gives an estimate of the diffusion coefficient for the ionic charge carriers in three different electrolyte systems, as per the Randel-Sevcikk equation [7]:
(7)
i p
= 2.69 ×
10 5
n
3 2
A
D
1 2
c
υ
1 2
Where ‘ip’is the anodic peak current in Ampere, ‘n’ is the number of electrons involved in the redox process, ‘A’ is the area of the electrode in cm2, ‘D’ is the diffusion coefficient in cm2/s, ‘c’ is the concentration of electroactive species in mol/cm3 and ‘υ’ is the voltage sweep rate in Vs−1. The diffusion coefficient for all the three electrolyte systems can be approximated from the slope of the i p vs. υ1/2 graph as shown in Fig. 9(e), the diffusion coefficient is highest when 1M KOH is used as an electrolyte (Table 1) which implies that the passivation layer thickness is least in the case of 1M KOH as an electrolyte. Fig. 9(f) shows the comparison of the CV plots of 1M KOH with 3M KOH, it confirms that in the case of 3M KOH as an electrolyte, the peak currents are reduced in comparison to 1M KOH as the electrolyte, this can be ascribed to the greater thickness of the passivation layer with the increased concentration of electrolyte, which is known to decrease the reactivity of electrode due to the deposition of the film on its surface.

The specific capacity for the LFP cathode under the different electrolytes was determined by galvanostatic charge/discharge testing in the constant current mode where the battery is subjected to a load that draws a constant current (1mA in this case) for a measurable quantity of time in which the battery reaches its predefined cut-off voltage. Specific capacity for the LFP electrode with the three electrolytesis shown in Fig. 11 (a–c) and Table 1. It is observed that the as-prepared LFP cathode with 1M KOH as an electrolyte shows the largest charge-discharge capacity among all. The voltage difference ΔV between the charge-discharge Plateaus indicates the polarization of the cell system [45,46]. Among the three electrolyte cell systems, 1M KOH has the smallest ΔV,which indicates the least cell polarization, consistent with EIS analysis observations. The low discharge capacity for the Na2SO4 electrolyte can be attributed to incomplete oxidation of the FePO4 during the reduction process [40]. Table 2 shows the nomenclature of the reporting symbols used in the equations and their unit of measurement in the present work.

The results show that 1M KOH gives the highest anodic peak current of 8.85 mA as compared to 4.8 mA for 2M NaOH and 0.6 mA for Na2SO4. Charge-transfer resistance, Rct is found to be 0.2 Ω, 0.3 Ω and 1.8 Ω for 1M KOH, 2M NaOH, 1M Na2SO4, respectively. Ionic diffusion coefficient is observed 3.24 × 10−14, 9.02 × 10−15, 8.46 × 10−17, for 1M KOH, 2M NaOH, 1M Na2SO4, respectively. Solution resistance, Rsol is found to be 1.7Ω, 1.3Ω, 2.9Ω for 1M KOH, 2M NaOH, 1M Na2SO4, respectively. The specific capacity of LiFePO4 cells with these aqueous electrolytes is very low, further analysis of the factors responsible for the capacity fading can give us more insight into ways to use aqueous electrolytes for LiFePO4 electrochemical cells, which would make it cheaper and safer.

In summary, the hydrothermal synthesis route has successfully synthesized LiFePO4 (LFP). The structural conformity of which was obtained by XRD, FTIR, and Raman analysis of the sample. XRD analysis exhibit orthorhombic olivine structure with Pbnm (62) space group and lattice parameters a = 4.98 Å, b = 10.04 Å, and c = 5.89 Å. FESEM imaging of the LFP reveals the disc type morphology with visible agglomeration leading to a particle size distribution in the range of 150 nm -600 nm.A systematic investigation was conducted on the electrochemical performance of LFPcathode material under three different aqueous electrolytes that are 2M NaOH, 1M Na2SO4, and 1M KOH. From the EIS analysis, it is seen that 2M NaOH as an electrolyte offers the least solution resistance, but its specific capacity and peak current are lower than1M KOH electrolyte. While Na2SO4 as an electrolyte transforms LFP cathode into olivine-structured NaFePO4, which has a second point of inflection for the anodic voltage sweep corresponding to its two-step phase change, further incomplete oxidation during the discharge process leads to low specific capacity in this case. Whereas the LFP cathode with 1M KOH as an electrolyte has the maximum peak current, diffusivity, and highest specific capacity, the charge transfer resistance Rct and solution resistance Rsol is almost equivalent to the 2M NaOH values, it is estimated to haveredox reversibility which is only slightly less than the case when 2M NaOH is used as an electrolyte, but it has also had the least polarization resistance among the three electrolytes. It is also observed that KOH's reactivity as an electrolyte is reduced when its concentration is increased to 3M from 1M. Hence, it is concluded that in the case of 1M KOH used as an electrolyte for the as-prepared LFP cathode, we observe optimal electrochemical activity in comparison to 2M NaOH and 1M Na2SO4 as electrolytes.A systematic study of the electrochemical behavior of as-synthesized LEP cathode with different aqueous electrolytes reveals that 1M KOH as an electrolyte is best suited among the other electrolytes taken in the present work. Thus, the investigation revealed thatthe selection of electrolytes plays a significant role in tuning the electrochemical properties of LiFePO4 (LFP) cathode material, and accordingly for the performance of LIBs applications.

Sukesh Kumar: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Prakash Chand: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Validation, Resources, Supervision. Anand Kumar: Software, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Hardeep Anand: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: One of the authors, P. Chand, is thankful to the Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB), Govt. of India, for providing funding through research project No: SERB/F/10804/2017-18. The authors are also thankful to the Director, NIT Kurukshetra, for providing the facilities in the Physics Department.
